steps.prompt: Which words form an imperative prohibition?

hints.pusillus grex: 'Pusillus grex' means 'little flock' — the prohibition is 'Nolite timere'
hints.timere pusillus: 'Timere' is the infinitive and 'pusillus' is an adjective — they don't form a prohibition together
hints.Nolite grex: 'Nolite' needs an infinitive to form the prohibition — 'timere' is that infinitive

hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be a statement — 'Nolite' is a command
hints.present subjunctive: Subjunctive would be 'nolitis' — 'Nolite' is imperative plural
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'noluistis' — 'Nolite' is present imperative

hints.volo, velle, volui, —: These are the parts of 'velle' (to wish) — 'nolle' is its negative compound
hints.malo, malle, malui, —: These are the parts of 'malle' (to prefer)
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry/bear)

hints.teneo, tenere, tenui, tentum: These are the parts of 'tenere' (to hold)
hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.moneo, monere, monui, monitum: These are the parts of 'monere' (to warn)

steps.reveal: pusillus = little (voc. masc. sg., addressing the flock)

hints.nominative: While 'pusillus' looks identical in nominative, the flock is being addressed — vocative
While 'grex' looks identical in nominative, Jesus is addressing the flock — vocative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'gregis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'gregis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'gregis' ends in -is
